coredata.dc:title: Preparation of perylene diimide modified AgCl photocatalyst and its photocatalytic performance for degrading various organic pollutants under visible light
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Shuting
Wang, Zhimeng
Yao, Lizhu
Shi, Lei
coredata.dc:description: A new type of perylene diimide attached AgCl photocatalyst (PDISA/AgCl) was prepared by one-step chemical precipitation method. Some characterizations, like XRD, SEM and XPS, etc, were measured to analyze its crystal structure, surface characteristics, elemental composition. From the characterization results, PDISA/AgCl exhibited broader light absorption range and higher separation efficiency of photoinduced charge. During the photodegradation process, PDISA/AgCl composites can effectively degrade rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), especially in the degradation of RhB, it shows excellent stability performance. Therefore, the PDISA/AgCl composite is an efficient and stable photocatalyst. Finally, the possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed. The prepared new photocatalyst show good application prospects in photodegradation of water pollution and environmental treatment.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalysis
AgCl
PDISA
Degradation of pollutants

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) AgCl, (b) PDISA/AgCl-40, (c) PDISA/AgCl-50, (d) PDISA/AgCl-60 and (e) PDISA.
The FTIR spectra of (a) AgCl, (b) PDISA/AgCl-40, (c) PDISA/AgCl-50, (d) PDISA/AgCl-60 and (e) PDISA.
XPS of AgCl, PDISA and PDISA/AgCl-50 composite: (A) full spectrum of PDISA/AgCl, (B) C 1 s, (C) N 1 s, (D) O 1 s, (E) Ag 3d and (F) Cl 2p.
SEM images of (A) AgCl, (B) PDISA, (C) PDISA/AgCl-40, (D) PDISA/AgCl-50 and (E) PDISA/AgCl-60.
(A) UV–vis DRS over (a) AgCl, (b) PDISA/AgCl-40, (c) PDISA/AgCl-50, (d) PDISA/AgCl-60 and (e) PDISA. Band gap of (B) AgCl and (C) PDISA.
(A) Transient photocurrent characteristics and (B) Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of AgCl, PDISA and PDISA/AgCl-50.

(A) Photodegradation curves of RhB under visible light; (B) First-order kinetic curves of RhB photodegradation; (C) Rate constant histogram of (a) PDISA/AgCl-40, (b) PDISA/AgCl-50, (c) PDISA/AgCl-60 and (d) AgCl; (D) Cycling experiments of photodegradation of RhB over PDISA/AgCl-50.
(A) XRD patterns and (B) FTIR of PDISA/AgCl-50 composite before and after photodegradation of RhB, (C) SEM image of used PDISA/AgCl-50.
(A) Active species capture assay on PDISA/AgCl-50 composite, (B) DMPO-·O2 - and (C) DMPO-·OH ESR spectroscopy of PDISA/AgCl-40、PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60 composite.
Photocatalytic mechanism of PDISA/AgCl composites under visible light.

head.ce:title: Preparation of perylene diimide modified AgCl photocatalyst and its photocatalytic performance for degrading various organic pollutants under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Liaoning Petrochemical University, Fushun 113001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Liaoning Petrochemical University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Petrochemical Engineering, Liaoning Petrochemical University, Fushun 113001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new type of perylene diimide attached AgCl photocatalyst (PDISA/AgCl) was prepared by one-step chemical precipitation method. Some characterizations, like XRD, SEM and XPS, etc, were measured to analyze its crystal structure, surface characteristics, elemental composition. From the characterization results, PDISA/AgCl exhibited broader light absorption range and higher separation efficiency of photoinduced charge. During the photodegradation process, PDISA/AgCl composites can effectively degrade rhodamine B (RhB), methyl orange (MO), methylene blue (MB) and tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), especially in the degradation of RhB, it shows excellent stability performance. Therefore, the PDISA/AgCl composite is an efficient and stable photocatalyst. Finally, the possible photocatalytic mechanism is proposed. The prepared new photocatalyst show good application prospects in photodegradation of water pollution and environmental treatment.

PDISA/AgCl photocatalyst with excellent photocatalytic performance under visible light was prepared, and its photocatalytic degradation mechanism was proposed.
ga1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, incomplete treatment of domestic and industrial wastewater and the burning of fossil fuels have caused serious water pollution [1,2] and air pollution [3,4]. Physical adsorption [5], ozone oxidation [6] and ion exchange [7] have been applied for treating these problems. However, in terms of energy saving and emission reduction, it is necessary to use clean energy to solve above problems. Therefore, photocatalysis has attracted interest, because solar energy could be used to degrade pollutants [8,9], produce H2 [10,11], reduce CO2 [12,13].

Recently, silver halide photocatalysts have attracted much attention because of their good photodegradation ability [14–17]. In particular, AgCl-based photocatalysts exhibit good photocatalytic properties [18–20]. However, higher photogenerated carrier complexes and weaker photoresponsiveness affect its performance. According to the available literature [21], silver halides could exhibit strong stability and photocatalytic activity when they were combined with other photocatalysts [22,23]. Obviously, to improve its performance, building composite is an effective method.

Organic photocatalytic materials have the advantages of diverse structures, excellent optical properties, and low cost [24]. Perylene dimethyl (PDISA), an organic supramolecular material formed by self-assembly of perylene diimide (PDI) [25], is a typical n-type semiconductor photocatalyst with good photoresponsiveness, which not merely displays good photocatalytic properties, and it can be used to modify other materials. When PDISA is compounded with other materials, it is able to form great interfacial contacts with other materials and induce the separation of photogenerated carriers [8,26–28]. Nevertheless, there are few reports about PDISA modified Ag-based semiconductors.

In this study, PDISA was successfully attached to AgCl surface by one-step chemical self-assembly process to form a new photocatalyst (PDISA/AgCl). Four pollutants, RhB, MB, MO and TC, were selected to simulate pollutant models to measure the photocatalytic property of PDISA/AgCl composites. The results reveal that the photodegradation performance of PDISA/AgCl composite is significantly better than that of bare AgCl under the same conditions, which attributed to more effectively separated rate of photogenerated electrons and holes, and stronger light response capacity. Hence, an efficient AgCl based photocatalyst was prepared.
PDI was prepared according to previous reports [29,30]. The detailed steps were shown in Supporting Information.

0.01 g of PDI was added in 3.85 mL of deionized water, which formed a turbid solution. After 15.38 μL of triethylamine was added, the above solution formed homogeneous solution, and dropped 0.67 mL of 4 mol/L hydrochloric acid to form a self-assembled PDI [28]. The solution was stirred for 1 h and then 0.5926 g of silver nitrate was added. After that, the sample was stirred for 3 h. The obtained sample was centrifuged, washed, and finally dried. The obtained sample was marked as PDISA/AgCl-50, where 50 presents the mass ratio of AgCl to PDISA 50:1. The PDISA/AgCl-40 and PDISA/AgCl-60 were also prepared by changing the mass of PDISA. AgCl was prepared through above process except the addition of PDI and triethylamine.

The detailed information of instruments, including X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform-infrared (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM), Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM), Ultraviolet-Visible Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS) and Electron Spin Resonance (ESR), etc, photocatalytic degradation experiment and electrochemical measurement were shown in Supporting Information.

Fig. 1 shows that the XRD patterns of AgCl, PDISA/AgCl-40, PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60. AgCl and PDISA/AgCl composites have narrow half-peak widths, indicating good crystallinity. In Fig. 1(a), the diffraction peaks correspond to crystal plans of AgCl, which is consistent with the XRD standard card of AgCl (JCPDS No. 31–1238) [31]. In addition, the PDISA/AgCl composites have the same peak position and similar diffraction peaks compared with bare AgCl, indicating that the crystal structure of AgCl is stable and not change due to the addition of PDISA. The diffraction peaks of PDISA are not obvious, which may be due to the low crystallinity of PDISA. Therefore, there is no PDISA diffraction peak in the PDISA/AgCl composite. The presence of PDISA in PDISA/AgCl composites was evidenced by subsequent FTIR tests.

The FTIR of AgCl, PDISA, PDISA/AgCl-40, PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60 were shown in Fig. 2. For bare AgCl, it is an inorganic compound and has no characteristic signal peaks. The FTIR of as-prepared PDISA/AgCl composites are basically consistent with that of PDISA. The peaks located at 1680 and 1650 cm−1 are respective the CO and CC stretching peaks in the benzene ring [32]. The peaks at 1590 and 1342 cm−1 are stretching of perylene ring [33]. The peak at 1400 cm−1 belongs to the amide group. The peaks at 1030–1240 cm−1 are attributed to -N-CH2, which are the characteristic peaks of PDI, indicating the successful preparation of PDI [34]. The characteristic peaks of PDISA/AgCl composites contain all the characteristic peaks of PDISA, indicating that the chemical structure of PDISA is stable and PDISA is introduced into the PDISA/AgCl composites. In addition, the peak intensity of PDISA is stronger than as-prepared PDISA/AgCl composites, which might attribute to the lower amount of PDISA in PDISA/AgCl composites.

Then PDISA/AgCl-50 composite was characterized by XPS test. Fig. 3(A) shows that the full XPS spectrum of PDISA/AgCl-50. There are five elemental characteristic peaks. They are respective C, N, O, Ag and Cl, which indicate the high purity of the prepared AgCl/PDI composites. Fig. 3 (B-D) shows the binding energies of C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s in pure PDISA and PDISA/AgCl-50 composite, and it can be seen that the binding energies are consistent. In Fig. 3(B), there are 4 obvious peaks in the C 1 s spectrum, the peak at 284.6 eV belongs to CC, the peaks at 287.6, 288.9 and 291.9 eV belong to C-C or C-N, CO and OC-O, respectively. Fig. 3(C) is N 1 s spectrum, showing that a characteristic peak at 399.9 eV presents C-N. In Fig. 3(D), there are 3 peaks in the spectrum of O 1 s, the peaks located at the binding energies of 531.1, 532.2 and 533.1 eV correspond to CO, C-OH and O-CO, respectively. Fig. 3(E, F) shows the binding energies of Ag+ and Cl- in pure AgCl and PDISA/AgCl-50 composite, and there is slight binding energy shift, which reveals that there is an interaction between PDISA and AgCl [35]. Fig. 3(E) shows the spectrum of Ag 3d. The two peaks at 373.3 eV and 367.3 eV in pure AgCl and the peak at 373.1 eV and 367.1 in PDISA/AgCl-50 composite are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Ag 3d3/2 and Ag 3d5/2 of Ag+ in AgCl, respectively [31]. Fig. 3(F) shows two peaks of Cl- in AgCl. The two peaks at 197.6 eV and 199.2 eV in pure AgCl and the peaks at 197.4 eV and 199.0 eV in PDISA/AgCl-50 composite are attributed to the characteristic peaks of Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2 of Cl-, respectively [36].

The morphologies of AgCl, PDISA and PDISA/AgCl-40, PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60 were observed by SEM. In Fig. 4(A), AgCl is granular in different sizes and its surface is smooth. In Fig. 4(B), PDISA is rod-shaped. Fig. 4(C-E) are the SEM images of PDISA/AgCl-40, PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60, it is found that the external rod-shaped PDISA tightly adhered to the AgCl particles, resulting that the surfaces of AgCl particles become rough. When the mass of AgCl is relatively excessive, the aggregation of PDISA rods on the surface of AgCl particles decreases. Obviously, attached PDISA could form tightly interface contact with AgCl particle, which might accelerate the transfer of photogenerated electrons, improving its photocatalytic efficiency. Then the TEM images of AgCl, PDISA and PDISA/AgCl-50 were also provided in Fig. S1. Obviously, agreeing with the results of SEM, AgCl and PDISA show particles and rod feature. For PDISA/AgCl-50, there are some nano-rod PDISA loaded on the surface of AgCl particles.

The optical properties of AgCl, PDISA and PDISA/AgCl composites were detected by DRS ( Fig. 5(A)). AgCl has an absorption intensity in visible light range, which may be due to the presence of a small amount of Ag [37]. Compared with AgCl, the light absorption capacity of as-prepared PDISA/AgCl composites has been significantly improved, and as the loaded amount of PDISA increases, the light absorption capacities of as-prepared PDISA/AgCl composites gradually enhance. In Fig. 5(B, C) Kubelka-Munk diagram [38], the band gap width (Eg) of AgCl is estimated to be 2.99 eV [14], PDISA is 1.66 eV [39].

Photoluminescence (PL) could reflect the recombination and separation of photogenerated electrons and holes [40]. Fig. S2 shows the PL spectra of AgCl、PDISA and PDISA/AgCl-50. The bare AgCl showed strong fluorescence spectral peaks, indicating that the carrier recombination rate in AgCl is high. The electron-hole recombination rate in PDISA is low, and the fluorescence spectrum peak is weak. However, the fluorescence spectrum of the PDISA/AgCl-50 composite is significantly lower than that of AgCl. It means that the photogenerated charges are effectively migrated between PDISA and AgCl, resulting in fluorescence quenching, which is beneficial to improve photocatalytic activity.

The photocurrent can also characterize the carrier separation efficiency [41]. The stronger the signal peak of the photocurrent, the higher the separation rate of the photoinduced charges. In Fig. 6(A), PDISA/AgCl-50 has a significantly higher photocurrent density than PDISA and AgCl, indicating that the PDISA/AgCl-50 composite can show better the separation of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [42]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) can represent effective charge transfer [43]. The size of the arc radius directly reflects the charge transfer resistance. In Fig. 6(B), the arc radius of PDISA/AgCl composite is smaller than that of bare PDISA and AgCl, indicating that the resistance during the charge transfer process could be reduced. It is further proved that the PDISA/AgCl composite can promote electron and hole separation, which is beneficial to charge migration.

The photocatalytic performance of AgCl and PDISA/AgCl composites was examined by degrading RhB. Fig. 7(A) shows the degradation curves of 10 mg/L RhB over AgCl and PDISA/AgCl composites. The photocatalytic activity of PDISA/AgCl composites has been significantly improved compared with bare AgCl, indicating that the addition of PDISA can improve the photodegradation ability of AgCl. Among them, the degraded ability of the PDISA/AgCl-50 composite was the highest, its degradation rate reached 90%. Fig. S3 detects the total organic carbon content (TOC). It can be seen that the TOC of Rhodamine B solution decreased by 18% after photodegradation reaction for 2 h. It is proved that the prepared photocatalyst can mineralize RhB dye. Then Fig. 7(B-C) and Table S1 show first-order kinetic model and rate constants (k). The k of AgCl, PDISA/AgCl-40, PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60 are respective 0.0165, 0.0934, 0.1140 and 0.0923 min−1, where the k of PDISA/AgCl-50 is the highest, which is 6.91 times that of bare AgCl. The stability of the photocatalyst was evaluated by recycling degrade 10 mg/L rhodamine B over PDISA/AgCl-50 photocatalyst. Fig. 7(D) shows three times degradation cycle tests of PDISA/AgCl-50 composite, and it can be seen that PDISA/AgCl-50 exhibited excellent photocatalytic performance after cycle tests. Obviously, the prepared PDISA/AgCl photocatalyst has stable and good photocatalytic performance.

To better understand the change of PDISA/AgCl composite before and after the degradation experiment, XRD was performed. As can be seen in Fig. 8(A), compared to the fresh catalyst, the cycled photocatalyst showed a new peak at 38.1°, Which attributed to the (111) crystal plane of Ag [44], marked with "*", which indicated that photocorrosion of AgCl and the decrease of AgCl content in PDISA/AgCl-50 composites lead to the decrease of peak intensity in XRD after cycling [45]. The FTIR spectra before and after cycling (Fig. 8(B)) had no significant changes, suggesting that the chemical structure of PDISA in the PDISA/AgCl composite did not change after recycling experiments. From the SEM after cycling (Fig. 8(C)), it can be seen that compared with fresh catalyst, the surface feature of used PDISA/AgCl did not change.

To determine whether PDISA/AgCl composites show photocatalytic degradation properties for other pollutants, AgCl and PDISA/AgCl-50 composite were also investigated to degrade MB (Fig. S4(A, B)), MO (Fig. S4 (C, D)) and TC (Fig. S4 (E, F)). Under the same conditions, the PDISA/AgCl-50 composite showed superior photocatalytic degradation ability compared to AgCl. In Fig. S4 (B), the degradation rate constant of bare AgCl for MB is 0.02401 min−1 and that of PDISA/AgCl-50 is 0.08141 min−1, and the photodegradation rate is increased by 3.39 times. In Fig. S4 (D), the degradation rate constant of MO over AgCl is 0.02273 min−1, and that of PDISA/AgCl-50 is 0.07612 min−1. In Fig. S4 (F), the degradation rate constant of AgCl for TC is 0.03122 min−1 and that of PDISA/AgCl-50 is 0.07803 min−1, with a 2.50-fold increase in the photodegradation rate. Comparing the degradation rates of AgCl and PDISA/AgCl-50 composite for the four simulated pollutants, the degradation effect of RhB is better, probably because RhB might exist certain dye sensitization effect.

The active species were identified by the addition of different scavengers. The disodium oxalate tetraacetate (EDTA-2Na), ascorbic acid (AA), methanol (MeOH) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) are holes (h+), superoxide radicals(·O2 -), hydroxyl radical (·OH) and photogenerated electron (e-) scavenger [46–48], respectively. In Fig. 9, after adding AA, the degradation rate of PDISA/AgCl-50 composites decreased from 90.21% to 32.48%, indicating that·O2 - was the main active species. After adding EDTA-2Na, DMSO and MeOH, the degradation rate decreased to 60.57%, 67.26% and 74.06%, respectively, indicating that·OH, e- and h+ also played a minor role.

In order to verify that PDISA/AgCl composites generate superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals during photocatalysis degradation process, DMPO was used as a trapping agent to conduct ESR tests on the PDISA/AgCl-40、PDISA/AgCl-50 and PDISA/AgCl-60 composites. In Fig. 9(B, C), under the dark reaction conditions, no signal peaks for generating free radicals were detected, while obvious signal peaks for superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals were detected for the three composite materials after the same time of illumination [49]. Among them, PDISA/AgCl-50 produced a greatest peak intensity of free radicals, which revealed that PDISA/AgCl-50 could form the most active species under the same conditions, hence, PDISA/AgCl-50 became the optimal photocatalyst. Meanwhile, it was confirmed that PDISA/AgCl composites could generate superoxide radicals and hydroxyl radicals under light conditions.

Since the activity of photocatalyst is related to its semiconductor type, conduction band (CB) and valence band (VB), the band level and semiconductor type can be determined by using Mott-Schottky measurements. As can be seen in Fig. S5, the Mott-Schottky curves of bare AgCl and PDISA at frequencies of 1000 Hz, 1500 Hz and 2000 Hz were measured. The slopes of the tangent lines are positive, meaning that they are n-type semiconductors and have the characteristics of n-type semiconductors. According to the Mott-Schotty formula, the flat band potential (Efb) of AgCl and PDISA with respect to the Ag/AgCl electrode can be determined as − 0.13 VSCE and − 0.39 VSCE, respectively. The CB potential of the n-type semiconductor is negative 0.1 V over Efb [50], and the Efb relative to the normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) is − 0.03 VNHE and − 0.29 VNHE, respectively, obtained from the equation ENHE=EAg/AgCl+ 0.197 V [51]. According to the equation EVB =ECB+Eg [38], the VB potential of AgCl and PDISA are 2.96 VNHE and 1.37 VNHE, respectively.

Combined with above experimental results, it is inferred the photocatalytic mechanism of PDISA/AgCl composites. The CB and VB of AgCl and PDISA are shown in Fig. 10. At the beginning of the reaction (Fig. 10A), the CB of PDISA is more negative than the CB of AgCl, so e- generated on PDISA is rapidly moved to the CB of AgCl, which is lower than O2/·O2 - (+0.13 VNHE), therefore e- enriched on AgCl reacts with O2 to generate superoxide radicals (·O2 -),·O2 - may generate·OH after a multi-step reaction. The VB of AgCl is more positive than that of PDISA, so h+ can move from AgCl to PDISA, but the VB of PDISA is lower than·OH/H2O(+2.68 VNHE) [52], therefore, h+ enriched on the surface of PDISA cannot oxidize water to·OH, and h+ will directly participate in the degradation of pollutants. The results are consistent with the active species capture test. As the photocatalytic degradation reaction goes on (Fig. 10B), e- could reduce Ag+ to Ag, which also accumulated e- to promote the separation of photoinduced electrons and holes, and facilitating the formation of·O2 - and·OH [53].·O2 -, h+ and·OH degrade pollutants. This process effectively limits the recombination of photogenerated charges, improving the photodegradation performance.

A new PDISA/AgCl photocatalyst was obtained through one-step chemical precipitation method. The rod-shaped PDISA was attached to the granular AgCl surface, which resulted in the efficient separation of photoinduced charges and enhanced the photocatalytic ability. Compared with bare AgCl, the photodegradation rates of RhB, MO, MB and TC by PDISA/AgCl composites increased by 6.91, 3.35, 3.39 and 2.5 times, respectively, and maintained stable photodegradation performance in RhB experiments. Capture agent experiments showed that·O2 - was the key active species. This study clarifies the preparation method of high-efficiency photocatalysts and provides a novel material for photodegradation, which is expected to solve the problems of pollutant degradation.
Shuting Zhang: Methodology, Software, Writing − original draft. Zhimeng Wang: Data curation, Software. Lizhu Yao: Data curation, Software. Lei Shi: Conceptualization, Writing − review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the financial supported from talent scientific research fund of LSHU (2016XJJ-080) and scientific research funding project of Liaoning Provincial Department of Education (L2020038).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfa.2022.129902.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel AgCl-based visible-light photocatalyst through in-situ assembly of carbon dots for efficient dye degradation and hydrogen evolution
